active_answer: resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force when injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice
active_question: In Kinbaku Today’s “The Ghosts of Kinbaku History,” what does onnen mean?
edited_answer: Onnen means resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force because injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice.
edited_question: In Kinbaku Today’s “The Ghosts of Kinbaku History,” what does onnen mean?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement turns the source-supported definition into a complete answer while preserving its causal qualification.
reason: The replacement turns the source-supported definition into a complete answer while preserving its causal qualification.
support_evidence: The section title of the sixth section “A Wife’s Resentment” (女房の怨念) is crucial because onnen (怨念) does not simply name anger or resentment. It names resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force. In the logic of Japanese ghost stories, onnen is what remains when injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice. Here, Otori’s grief over her husband’s destruction and Umeko’s suffering under Iwazō’s debt violence become part of the same moral atmosphere: the wronged wife’s resentment gathers until it demands repayment.

active_question: In Kinbaku Today’s “Tsuji Banzuke,” what were the wood-printed theater posters that depicted a whole story sequentially called?
edited_answer: They were called Tsuji Banzuke (辻番付 or 辻番附).
edited_question: In Kinbaku Today’s “Tsuji Banzuke,” what were the wood-printed theater posters that depicted a whole story sequentially called?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement makes the source-supported name a direct answer and retains both Japanese written forms supplied by the evidence.
reason: The replacement makes the source-supported name a direct answer and retains both Japanese written forms supplied by the evidence.
support_evidence: Big theaters announced their programs through newspapers, however, small theaters employed posters for their primary promotion because of their small budget. “Tsuji Banzuke”(辻番付, 辻番附) were wood printed theater posters in which scenes of plays were sequentially depicted so that people could get a whole story at a glance. It was posted at public baths, barbershops etc. where people gathered.
